attributes.stem:
A 56-year old man undergoes a laparoscopic cholecystectomy two days after being admitted with acute cholecystitis. At operation, some inflammatory changes are found around the gallbladder, which makes the procedure more difficult than expected. A drain is placed in the gallbladder bed at the end of the operation. The following day the patient does not look well and is complaining of significant right upper quadrant pain. He has required regular morphine overnight to control his pain.

His blood pressure is 120/70mmHg, respiratory rate of 18, pulse rate 100/min and temperature is 37.8C. He has passed reasonable amounts of urine since the operation. On pulmonary auscultation there are bibasal crackles but he is reticent to take deep breaths due to his abdominal discomfort. On abdominal examination there is involuntary guarding and tenderness in the right upper quadrant. Bowel sounds are present. Nothing has come out of the drain. A portable chest X-ray is ordered and shows diffuse bilateral coalescent opacities. His WCC is elevated, and LFTs show mildly elevated bilirubin, GGT and ALP.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following would be the most likely explanation for his current problem?
attributes.explanation:
This patient is clearly in pain post-operatively and given the history this is most likely due to a biliary leak causing localised peritonitis. He is unwell and this needs requires intervention. Bile leaks are possible after even a seemingly straightforward laparoscopic cholecystectomy and patients can be surprisingly well the first day after surgery and deteriorate rapidly thereafter when they develop more obvious peritonitis and potentially become septic. Drains do not always drain.

He needs resuscitation and it would be reasonable in a case as clear as this to perform repeat laparoscopy to confirm the diagnosis, wash out the bile, identify the leak if possible and deal with this accordingly or at least place better drains. If the diagnosis is in doubt and the patient is stable then a CT scan can confirm.

Aspiration pneumonia and pulmonary embolism are always possible post-operatively and should be part of any post-operative complication differential but the clinical picture is one of intra-abdominal pathology with pain and developing sepsis rather than respiratory compromise. The pulmonary findings of bibasal crackles, the low grade fever and the radiological changes are in keeping with basal atelectasis - a common post-operative situation and very unlikely to explain this patient’s problem.

Urinary retention is common post-operatively and this would cause abdominal pain. But this is more likely sited centrally or in the supra-pubic region and associated with decreased urine output.

Iatrogenic duodenal injury during laparoscopic cholecystectomy from thermal burns or dissection is certainly possible and would cause a similar picture but is not common and an early re-operation would rapidly identify this as the culprit. A concurrent duodenal ulcer is again possible but unlikely.
learningPoints: In a patient who becomes unwell soon after a surgical procedure, some complication directly related to that procedure must be considered.
references: Copelan A, Bahoura L, Tardy F, Kirsch M, Sokhandon F, Kapoor B. Etiology, Diagnosis, and Management of Bilomas: A Current Update. Tech Vasc Interv Radiol. 2015 Dec;18(4):236-43. doi: 10.1053/j.tvir.2015.07.007. Epub 2015 Jul 15. PMID: 26615164.

Nikpour, A. M., Knebel, R. J., & Cheng, D. (2016). Diagnosis and Management of Postoperative Biliary Leaks. Seminars in interventional radiology, 33(4), 307–312. https://doi.org/10.1055/s-0036-1592324
Fong ZV, Pitt HA, Strasberg SM, et al. Diminished Survival in Patients with Bile Leak and Ductal Injury: Management Strategy and Outcomes. J Am Coll Surg 2018; 226:568.
